executiveImpact.question: How many customers exist in more than one ERP?
executiveImpact.answer: 7 customer clusters span multiple ERP systems. The most notable is Friendly Food Qatar, which appears in Infor, Epicor, and Oracle. These cross-ERP clusters represent the highest MDM value.

executiveImpact.question: Which dimension had the biggest reduction?
executiveImpact.answer: Sales channels saw the biggest consolidation — 28 raw channel labels reduced to 12 standardized channels, a 57% reduction. Key Account, Fuel Stations, Self Service Stores, and several others were unified under MODERN TRADE and RETAIL.

executiveImpact.question: What’s the total revenue managed across all systems?
executiveImpact.answer: AED 763.4 million across all 4 ERPs. CBD (SAP) accounts for 92.1% at AED 702.8M, followed by BMB (Epicor) at 5.2%.

dataLandscape.question: Which ERP has the worst data quality?
dataLandscape.answer: Epicor has the most quality issues — 24 of 30 customer issues and all 23 product issues originate from Epicor. Common problems include placeholder records (UNFI, SPIN, Sample), generic cash customer entries, and employee records mixed with trade customers.

dataLandscape.question: How many countries are in the data?
dataLandscape.answer: 12 countries across all sources. UAE dominates with 602 of 688 customer records (87%). KSA is second with 41 records. The data spans Middle East, Asia, North America, and Africa.

dataLandscape.question: Show me the record distribution by source.
dataLandscape.answer: SAP contributes the most customer records: 412 of 688 (60%). Epicor is second with 216 (31%). Oracle has 44 and Infor has 16. For products, Epicor leads with 543 of 851 records.

matchingEngine.question: Show me the Lulu group — how many names were consolidated?
matchingEngine.answer: Lulu Group had 82 different raw names across SAP consolidated into 6 golden entities, representing AED 83.9M in revenue. The largest — LULU HYPERMARKET — absorbed 53 name variations worth AED 67.6M. This is a 93% consolidation rate.

matchingEngine.question: What’s the difference between exact and fuzzy matching?
matchingEngine.answer: Exact matching finds records with identical core names and countries after the 8-step cleaning pipeline — 179 pairs found with 100% confidence. Fuzzy matching uses a composite score (Token Sort Ratio x 0.5 + Token Set Ratio x 0.3 + Jaccard x 0.2) to find near-matches — 21 pairs found with confidence between 0.85 and 0.96.

matchingEngine.question: Which clusters span multiple ERP systems?
matchingEngine.answer: 7 customer clusters span multiple ERPs. Notable examples: Friendly Food Qatar (Infor + Epicor + Oracle), Al Foah (Epicor + Infor), Dubai Duty Free (Epicor + SAP), and Al Homaizi (Epicor + Oracle). For products, 6 Date Crown (Infor) <-> Al Foah (SAP) matches were found.

survivorshipRules.question: Why was SAP chosen as the primary source for Customer Name?
survivorshipRules.answer: The survivorship rule for Customer Name is ‘Highest Completeness’ — the system selects whichever source has the longest, most complete legal name. SAP typically wins because it maintains full legal entity names (e.g., ‘Majid Al Futtaim Hypermarkets LLC’) while Epicor often truncates.

survivorshipRules.question: What happens if I change the source priority order?
survivorshipRules.answer: If Oracle were ranked above SAP, approximately 14 golden records would change their primary source. Cross-ERP clusters where both SAP and Oracle contribute would see field-level changes in Address and Phone resolution.

survivorshipRules.question: Explain the Product master survivorship rules.
survivorshipRules.answer: Product survivorship is primarily rule-based rather than source-priority driven. Brands are normalized via a 40+ variant dictionary. Categories are mapped to a 5-level enterprise hierarchy. Pack sizes are extracted via regex from descriptions. Quality flags are assigned by detection rules (INVALID for rebates/services, INCOMPLETE for missing fields).

goldenRecord.question: Walk me through how this golden record was created.
goldenRecord.answer: The pipeline starts at Bronze (raw ingestion with profiling), then Silver (8 cleaning steps: text normalization, special chars, legal suffix removal, abbreviation expansion, noise words, prefix normalization, duplicate word removal, core name extraction), then Gold (matching via exact/fuzzy, cluster formation, golden record election by quality > source trust > completeness > tiebreaker).

goldenRecord.question: How many sources contributed to this record?
goldenRecord.answer: This depends on the record. Most golden records come from a single source (the majority are single-member clusters). 7 customer clusters and 6 product pairs span multiple ERPs. The record profile panel on the right shows source contribution for the selected record.

goldenRecord.question: Show me the audit trail for AL BARAKAH.
goldenRecord.answer: AL BARAKAH (GCID-00004) has 2 cluster members — both from Infor. ‘AL BARAKAH DATES FACTORY LLC’ matched with ‘AL BARAKH DATES TR. LLC’ via fuzzy match (0.89 confidence). Key transformations: S3 stripped legal suffixes, S4 expanded TR to TRADING, S5 removed noise word TRADING, S8 extracted core name AL BARAKAH/AL BARAKH. The fuzzy matcher caught the spelling variation.
